title: Toffee apple
subtitle: Crisp caramel-coated apples on sticks
excerpt: Eating apples dipped in a hard-crack sugar syrup coloured with a drop of red food colouring, then set on sticks. A British fairground and bonfire night classic that requires a sugar thermometer and speed at the dipping stage.
sourceNotes: Toffee apples are a traditional British autumn confection, sold at fairgrounds and bonfire night celebrations. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, documents boiled sugar coatings for fruit under the confectionery chapter. The red-coloured hard-toffee apple coating became a fairground staple in the late nineteenth century.

content.text: A toffee apple works because the apple is cold and dry: the hot caramel hits the surface and sets instantly into a glassy shell. The two things that break this are moisture (a wet apple makes the coating slip) and timing (dipping before the sugar reaches the right temperature gives a soft coating that never properly hardens). Both are easy to avoid. Wipe the apples completely dry and watch the thermometer.
The caramel reaches 150°C. Keep children at a safe distance during cooking and dipping. If hot sugar contacts skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes.

attrs.prepNote: small, wiped completely dry, stalks removed

content.text: Prepare apples and cook the toffee

Push a wooden skewer or lolly stick firmly through the stalk end of each apple. Line a baking tray with baking paper and set it near the hob. Put the {{granulated-sugar}}, {{golden-syrup}}, {{white-wine-vinegar}}, and 100 ml of water in a medium saucepan. Stir over a low heat until the sugar dissolves, then stop stirring. Bring to a boil over medium-high heat and cook until the thermometer reads 150°C (hard-crack stage). Add the {{red-food-colouring}} if using and swirl the pan once to mix.

Working quickly and carefully, tilt the pan to pool the toffee on one side. Dip each apple into the toffee, rotating to coat all sides, and lift out, letting the excess drip back for a few seconds. Stand each coated apple on the lined tray. The toffee sets within a minute at room temperature. If the toffee in the pan thickens too fast, briefly return it to the heat. Leave the toffee apples to cool completely before eating.
